results.possibleIdentity: Jeremy D. Pollack was an engineer and electro-mechanical technician who was active at Rutgers University and the RCA David Sarnoff Research Center from the 1960s through the 2000s. In the mid-1960s, he was an Electro-Mechanical Technician in the Graduate Physics Lab at Rutgers University in New Brunswick, New Jersey, where he was involved in high-energy physics research and the implementation of computer control equipment, such as the LINC digital computer. He was a resident of the Highland Park and Somerset area of New Jersey for over 50 years. In 1971, he was listed in 'The Last Supplement to the Whole Earth Catalog,' a publication closely linked with the counter-culture and technical-art collaborations of the era, including Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). In 1975, Pollack joined RCA (later the Sarnoff Corporation), where he became a prominent researcher in video technology. He was a key contributor to the development of the Capacitance Electronic Disc (CED) VideoDisc system, for which he received an RCA Laboratories Achievement Award in 1979 for 'imaginative applications of microprocessor technology.' His later career at Sarnoff focused on advanced digital high-definition television (HDTV), and he held several patents related to video compression, playback mechanisms, and consumer electronics.
